coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-free and label-free detection of Staphylococcus aureus based on target-inhibited fluorescence signal recovery
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Shuo
Zhao, Chao
Shang, Mingyu
Li, Juan
Wang, Juan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a one-step fluorometric strategy based on nanometal surface energy transfer (NSET) between carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) was developed for facile detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The fluorescence of CDs was quenched up to 63.5% by AuNPs due to nucleic acid hybridization in the presence of linker DNA, which contained the complementary sequences of S. aureus-specific aptamer, and the fluorescence signal was in the “off” state. Upon aptamer addition, the CDs was released from linker DNA through the strong competitiveness of aptamer, leading to the notable fluorescence recovered. Once S. aureus is introduced, aptamer preferentially bind to the bacterial surface and cannot hybridize with complementary sequences in the linker DNA, resulting in the fluorescence signal with “off” state. Based on these findings, the performance and reliability of the fluorescence-based assay were evaluated. Compared to direct hybridization of complementary DNA on the surface of CDs and AuNPs, our sensing strategy has enhanced detection limit (10 cfu⋅mL−1) and improved linear range (10 to 106 cfu⋅mL−1) for S. aureus. Therefore, our proposed enzyme-free and label-free strategy may become a promising method for ease of operation, sensitive and selective S. aureus detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food safety
Foodborne pathogen
Fluorescence detection
Nanometal surface energy transfer
Gold nanoparticles
Carbon dots

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Nanomaterial-based technologies for determination of food toxicity, Edited by: Dr. Saura Sahu, Dr. Alireza Khataee, Dr. Amin Mousavi Khaneghah and Dr. Yasser Vasseghian

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The procedure of S. aureus detection
Detection of S. aureus in spiked samples
Construction of the NEST nanoprobes for S. aureus detection
Characterization of mode I and mode II nanoprobes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of different solutions: (I) CDs, (II) Mode I nanoprobes, (III) Mode I nanoprobes with 1 μM aptamer, (IV) Mode II nanoprobes, (V) Mode II nanoprobes with 1 μM aptamer. The inset images correspond to the visual fluorescent response.
TEM image of (a) mode I nanoprobes, (b) mode II nanoprobes, (b) mode I nanoprobes with aptamer and (d) mode II nanoprobes with aptamer.

Optimization of experimental conditions. (a) The value of ΔI/I0 in the presence of different volume ratio of DNA-CDs, (b) The value of ΔI/I0 in the different incubation time at room temperature. (c) The value of ΔI in the presence of different concentration of aptamers in sensing system. The value of ΔI with (d) prolonging the reaction time, (e) various value of pH, and (F) various temperature. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicates.

LOD relevant results for mode I nanoprobes (a) The image of this sensing system with different concentration of S. aureus, (b) Fluorescence spectra in the presence of different S. aureus (0, 10, 102, 103, 104, 105, and 106 cfu/mL), (c) The standard curve for S. aureus. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicates.

Fluorescence intensity of mode I and mode II nanoprobes in the presence of different analytes (S. typhimurium, L. monocytogenes, E. coli O157:H7, V. parahaemolyticus and S. Bogdii, each at 105 cfu/mL, S.aureus at 104 cfu/mL). Error bars represent the standard deviation of 10 replicates.
Schematic illustration of S. aureus detection using AuNPs and CDs.
Photoluminescence Emission Lifetime (ns) (τ) of CDs, mode I and mode II nanoprobes.

The recovery and RSD value of detecting various concentrations of S. aureus for mode I probe and mode II probe in spiked milk samples (
x ‾
± s
, n = 10).

head.ce:title: Enzyme-free and label-free detection of Staphylococcus aureus based on target-inhibited fluorescence signal recovery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Earth Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Earth Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling Editor: Dr. Jose Luis Domingo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a one-step fluorometric strategy based on nanometal surface energy transfer (NSET) between carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) was developed for facile detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The fluorescence of CDs was quenched up to 63.5% by AuNPs due to nucleic acid hybridization in the presence of linker DNA, which contained the complementary sequences of S. aureus-specific aptamer, and the fluorescence signal was in the “off” state. Upon aptamer addition, the CDs was released from linker DNA through the strong competitiveness of aptamer, leading to the notable fluorescence recovered. Once S. aureus is introduced, aptamer preferentially bind to the bacterial surface and cannot hybridize with complementary sequences in the linker DNA, resulting in the fluorescence signal with “off” state. Based on these findings, the performance and reliability of the fluorescence-based assay were evaluated. Compared to direct hybridization of complementary DNA on the surface of CDs and AuNPs, our sensing strategy has enhanced detection limit (10 cfu⋅mL−1) and improved linear range (10 to 106 cfu⋅mL−1) for S. aureus. Therefore, our proposed enzyme-free and label-free strategy may become a promising method for ease of operation, sensitive and selective S. aureus detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is a kind of notorious foodborne pathogenic bacterium with the ability to cause human bacterial gastroenteritis and other severe foodborne diseases worldwide (Le Loir et al., 2003), which represents a great threat to human health (Soriano et al., 2002). S. aureus can produce heat-resistant toxins in food and is widely distributed in meat, poultry, eggs, aquatic products, and dairy products (Trncikova et al., 2009). According to the USDA's economic research service report, the infectious foodborne illness caused by Staphylococcus food poisoning is over 180 thousand cases annually in US(Leonard et al., 2003).Therefore, rapid and early detection of S. aureus is essential for food safety. In general, S. aureus are detected by culture and biochemical test (Park and Choi, 2017), polymerase chain reaction (PCR) method (Malorny et al., 2008) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA)(Seo et al., 2010), but these methods are limited by their inherent shortcomings. Since these conventional methods are labor-intensive, time-consuming, require expert technicians, even have false-negative results (Carbonnelle et al., 2007) and non-specific interaction between protein G from S. aureus and the Fc region of mammalian immunoglobulin G (Zhang et al., 2019), more effective methods, including electrochemical, optical, thermal, and mass-based transducers, have been investigated. Among these effective methods, biosensor-based detection, such as carbon nanomaterial-based electrochemical biosensors (Muniandy et al., 2019), surface plasmon pesonance imaging based immunoassay biosensing (Wang and Park, 2020), is very promising because it does not require any pre-enrichment, it is cost effective and simple (Bhunia, 2008).

Recently, due to its easy operation, rapid response and remarkable selectivity, fluorescence biosensor technology has been widely used for determination of different analytes, such as metal ions (Tumay et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2021; Tumay and Yesilot, 2019; Uslu et al., 2020), nitroaromatic compounds (Tumay et al., 2020b) and biomolecules (Okutan et al., 2016; Tümay and Yeşilot, 2021). Among them, fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), as a non-radiative energy transfer process between fluorescent molecules based on the dipole-dipole interactions, is widely used as a spectroscopic technique in biosensing applications (Ge et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2017). FRET has rapid response and multiplexing capacity even in small sample volumes (Pehlivan et al., 2019). The efficiency of the process is strongly dependent on the properties of donor and acceptor (Semih et al., 2019). Traditionally, FRET donors mainly include organic fluorescent dyes (Ong and Yang, 2017) and quantum dots (QDs)(Sapsford et al., 2011). However, conventional organic fluorescent dyes suffer from low brightness and severe photobleaching, thus hampering their practical applications (Alidagi et al., 2019; Ibisoglu et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2017). Besides, QDs require multiple synthesis steps (Xu et al., 2015, 2016) and have poor preservation of aliquots of reagent and incompatibility with various commercially media for embedding specimens (Korzhevskii et al., 2011), which limiting their application.

More recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorophore, have received much attention due to their unique physicochemical properties and diverse applications in the fields of bio-imaging, and sensing (Thakur et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2017). CDs are spherical-like carbon particles (or graphitic fragments) with sizes less than 10 nm. They have outstanding performance such as easy to synthesis, strong fluorescence emission, high water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, high photostability against photobleaching (Lin et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2007), which is an ideal FRET donor.

Furthermore, Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are considered to be a kind of highly efficient fluorescence quencher due to its large surface area to volume ratio, easy surface functionalization, and strong surface plasma absorption in NIR-to-IR region (Zhu et al., 2010). Therefore, AuNPs are usually employed as efficient energy acceptor, whose broad absorption range largely overlaps the emission spectrum of CDs (Oskoei et al., 2016), and some fluorescent biosensors have established for persistent organic pollutant (Bu et al., 2014) and imaging RNA (Qin et al., 2019). Based on these features, AuNPs and CDs were chosen to construct the detection system of S.aureus as FRET donor-acceptor pairs. To our best knowledge, the FRET-based sensing system consists of CDs and AuNPs is rarely used for the detection of S.aureus.

In this work, we designed two mode of fluorescence quenching nanoprobe (mode I and mode II nanoprobe) for enzyme-free and label-free detection of S. aureus. Compared to direct hybridization of complementary DNA on the surface of CDs and AuNPs, our sensing strategy has enhanced detection limit (10 cfu⋅mL−1) and improved linear range (10 to 106 cfu⋅mL−1) for S. aureus. We envision that the present facile one-step approach would be widely applied in the early detection of pathogenic microorganism.
Details of materials and the protocol for optimization of experimental conditions were described in electronic supplementary material (ESM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The procedure of S. aureus detection
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria samples with varying concentrations (0, 10, 102, 103, 104, 105 and 106 cfu/mL) were prepared by diluting the freshly cultured bacteria with sterile PBS. After optimization of experimental conditions, each S. aureus standard solution (50 μL) was added into the mixture solution with a total of 200 μL PBS solution containing 50 μL probe 1 and 25 nM S.aureus-specific aptamers. The mixture was suspended for 45 min at room temperature. The intensity of the solution was measured with a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan) at excitation 322 nm. The procedure of S.aureus by Mode II probe was similar with Mode I probe. The difference is that the incubation time with bacteria is 60 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S. aureus in spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the usefulness of our approach for detection of S. aureus in food samples, milk was purchased from a local supermarket. The food samples contaminated with S. aureus were prepared as follow: a total of 5 mL of milk mixed with 5 mL of sterilized PBS. The mixture was filtered through 0.22 μm filter, and the filtrate was collected and inoculated with S. aureus at concentrations of 10, 102, 103, 104, 105, and 106 cfu/mL. In contrast, the negative control of blank sample was also prepared in a similar way and tested to investigate the matrix effect of the milk content in the absorbance measuring system. The detection protocol was described in section detection of S. aureus, except that pure PBS buffer were replaced with milk samples.

All the data, except special label, are expressed as the means ± standard deviations (x‾±SD) of three parallel samples. Paired t-tests was conducted for statistical analysis using SPSS Statistics software (Version 22.0, IBM, USA). Statistical significance was set at 0.05.

In this study, CDs were synthesized with citric acid and cys by a simple facile hydrothermal method. This kind of CDs have abundant advantages in facile synthesis, low cost, easy modification, excellent water solubility, strong fluorescence, and very low toxicity (Zhang et al., 2018c), which is an ideal donor for FRET. Due to CDs have abundant carboxyl groups, they can be easily conjugated with DNA through EDC/NHS coupling chemistry to form CDs-oligo and nanoprobes. To form FRET system, an ideal acceptor also needs to select. AuNPs have large extinction coefficient and broad energy absorption bandwidth in visible range that overlap by emission spectrum of many organic dyes, QDs and CDs (Bagdeli et al., 2017), which is an ideal FRET acceptor. Furthermore, AuNPs have high surface to volume ratio and their surface can be modified by functional groups to make individual nanoparticles for biological application (Shi et al., 2015a). Besides, DNA is chosen for the linking of the donor and acceptor, because it is easy to construct nanoprobes and release the payloads from designer nanomaterials (Zhang et al., 2018a). After the conjugation of AuNPs and CDs by DNA, the donor and acceptor are in close proximity and hence FRET occurs. Based on these features, CDs and AuNPs were chosen as the energy donor–acceptor pairs to construct an enzyme-free and label-free detection methods of S. aureus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the NEST nanoprobes for S. aureus detection
ce:section.ce:para: In this assay, AuNPs was chosen as an acceptor and CDs was chosen as a donor to establish a sensing system. Strategy concept and process are shown in Scheme 1 , the mode I nanoprobe consisted of a thiol-terminated single-strand oligo I functionalized AuNPs (AuNPs-oligo I), an amino-terminated single-strand oligo I functionalized CDs (CDs-oligo I) and a linker DNA contained the complementary sequences of S. aureus-specific aptamer, which tethered to a double-strand DNA (dsDNA). Due to the approaching of AuNPs to CDs in nanoprobes, AuNPs as the electron receptor caused fluorescence quenching of CDs based on a potential electron transfer from CDs to AuNPs, and the fluorescence signal was in the “off” state. In the absence of S.aureus, the CDs was released from linker DNA through the strong competitiveness of aptamer, leading to the notable fluorescence recovered. As the aptamer/target complex is more stable than the aptamer/DNA duplex (Chen et al., 2008). While S. aureus is introduced, aptamer preferentially bind to the bacterial surface and can profoundly be inhibited to trigger the disassembly of CDs with the AuNPs, resulting in the fluorescence signal with “off” state. Consequently, the variable fluorescence response from intense blue fluorescence to non-fluorescence were directly proportional to increasing S. aureus concentration.

To obtain the best sensing performance for S. aureus detection, two types for fluorescence quenching were adopted and investigated. It is well known that the quenching effect of AuNPs is markedly distance-dependent (Qin et al., 2018). The distance between CDs and AuNPs in the two types of nanoprobes was precisely controlled by using the same length of the DNA base pairs to compare the active and quenching behavior better. Mode I nanoprobes were constructed through a DNA self-assembly by using CDs-oligo I, AuNPs-oligo I and linker DNA, which contained the complementary sequences of S. aureus-specific aptamer. In contrast, Mode II nanoprobes were direct hybridized by using CDs-oligo II and AuNPs-oligo II, which have a complementary sequence of aptamer. Because increasing number of mismatches of oligos can lead to loss of duplex stability and reduce quenching effect (for example, the quenching efficiency of single-base mismatched oligos and double-base mismatched oligos dropped to 37% and 22% respectively)(Shi et al., 2015b), single-base mismatched pair existed in the Mode II nanoprobes for follow-up study. As shown in Fig. 1 , CDs emit a strong blue fluorescence at 418 nm (curve I), which is consistent with previous results (Zhang et al., 2018b). The quantum yield of CDs is about 22%, using quinine sulfate (0.10 mol L−1 H2SO4 as a solvent, QY = 54%) as a reference. After formation of Mode I and Mode II nanoprobes, the fluorescence intensity was greatly quenched (curve II and IV). Conversely, in the presence of aptamer, most of the fluorescence of CDs was recovered (curve III and V), because the aptamer can hybridize with the its complementary sequence to form a stable duplex structure and trigger the release of CDs from the nanoprobes. Interestingly, the degree of the recovered fluorescence of Mode II nanoprobes was much less pronounced than that of Mode I nanoprobes, owing to their stable structure (Piao et al., 2008). The above results also can be easily observed by bare eye (see inset of Fig. 1). All these results demonstrated that our NEST nanoprobes were successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of mode I and mode II nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: Morphological features of CDs-oligo and AuNPs-oligo have been assessed instrumentally by the combination of Transmission electron microscope (TEM), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), Zeta potential investigations and FTIR spectra. Detailed characterization results of CDs-oligo and AuNPs-oligo were described in electronic supplementary material (ESM).

Finally, to validate the ability of Mode I and Mode II nanoprobes to interact with aptamer, TEM, DLS and Zeta potential investigations was performed to monitor complexation. As shown in Fig. 2c and d, when S. aureus-specific aptamer was added, free CDs were observed. Hydrated particle size of Mode I and Mode II nanoprobes with aptamer was decreased to 122.07 ± 0.67 nm and 104.35 ± 0.31 nm, respectively (Fig. S4e and S4h). Compared to individual nanoprobes, particle size was smaller (Fig. S4i), which demonstrated that CDs was dissociated successfully. These results indicate that S. aureus-specific aptamer was successfully hybridized to the linker DNA or CDs-oligo II through a strand displacement reaction. However, for mode II nanoprobes, there are still several CDs on the surface of AuNPs. This result shows that the linker-DNA can increase the efficiency of the strand displacement reaction, which facilitated the recovery of fluorescent signals. These results demonstrate that we synthesized AuNPs and CDs hybrid nanoprobes and bind specifically to aptamer, displacing DNA-functionalized CDs.

According to the theory of one-step NEST-based assay, a serious of experimental parameters was optimized. First, the volume ratio of AuNPs to CDs and their incubation time were measured in the quenching process. The quenching efficiency is expressed by ΔI/I
0 (ΔI = I
0
–I, where I
0 is the original fluorescence intensity of CDs, and I is the fluorescence intensity of CDs after quenching). Fig. 3 a showed the quenching efficiency of Mode I nanoprobes increased sharply until the volume ratio of AuNPs to CDs reached1:3 with a value of 63.5%. Next, the fluorescence quenching time was further optimized with different test intervals (30, 60, 90, 120, 150 min), and the results were shown in Fig. 3b. It can be seen that 120 min is the most suitable for fluorescence quenching, when it reached to stable state. The similar trend was observed by elevating Mode II nanoprobes, and the best performance was obtained by incubation of 1:2 of AuNPs to CDs for 120 min. Subsequently, other key reaction conditions were optimized by investigating ΔI (ΔI = I
0
– I, where I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of S.aureus, respectively). S. aureus at 106 cfu/mL was used as a model. As shown in Fig. 3c, the value of ΔI increased stepwise with increasing aptamer concentration, and reached the maximum fluorescence intensity recovered with 100 nM aptamer, whatever types of nanoprobes. The ΔI of the two types of nanoprobes dramatic increased during 45 min incubation, where thereafter, and even appeared to slightly decrease. On the basis of these results, the following experimental conditions were chosen to give the best results: (a) the volume ratio of AuNPs to CDs was 1:3 for Mode I nanoprobes and 1:2 for Mode II nanoprobes; (b) fluorescence quenching time: 120 min for both mode I and mode II nanoprobes; (c) concentration of aptamer: 100 nM for both mode I and mode II nanoprobes; (d) incubation time: 45 min for mode I nanoprobes and 60 min for mode II nanoprobes; (e) value of pH: 7.4 for both mode I and mode II nanoprobes; (f) reaction temperature: 25 °C for both mode I and mode II nanoprobes.

Under the optimized conditions, the sensitivity of the NEST-based mode I strategy was experimented. As shown in Fig. 4 a, when the concentration of S. aureus increased from 0 to 106 cfu mL−1, the blue fluorescence changes from very bright to very dim, suggesting that aptamer strongly interacts with the target prior to disassembly of nanoprobes. The fluorescence emission spectrum was also been recorded. The characteristic emission peak of CDs at 418 nm was decreased gradually (Fig. 4b). Thus, a corresponding fluorescence detection was performed by measuring the ratio of the I/I 0, where I is the fluorescence intensity of sensing system with different concentrations of S.aureus and I
0 is the fluorescence intensity of blank samples. The calibration curve showed a good linear relationship between the ratio of I/I 0 and the concentration of S. aureus from 10 to 106 cfu mL−1 as the following regression equation: I/I 0 = −0.1046lgC +0.9172, with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9624, where C is the amount of S. aureus in cfu·mL−1 (Fig. 4c). The detection limit is as low as 10 cfu mL−1 (The corresponding signal change was higher than three times of deviation of the signals of blank samples). For comparison, the performance of mode II strategy was also examined (Fig. S8). The limit of detection is 100 cfu mL−1, achieved with the linear range of 102 to 106 cfu mL−1. The above results indicated that the nanoprobes containing linker DNA between CDs and AuNPs indeed reduced the detection limit and improved linear range for S. aureus. The direct hybridization of complementary DNA on the surface of CDS and AuNPs increased duplex stability, and made it harder to replace the mis-matched sequence by aptamer and release of CDs. In addition, repeatability (intra-assay variation) was determined by repeating the assay over a short period of time and reproducibility (inter-assay variation) was were assessed by analyzing the calibration standards on six separate days. Six parallel samples were used for repeatability and reproducibility testing, at three different S.aureus concentrations (102, 104, 106 cfu/mL). Repeatability and reproducibility were expressed as relative standard deviation (RSD%). For mode I nanoprobes, Intra-assay precision ranged between 0.98% and 1.90% RSD, and the inter-assay accuracy varied between 1.09% and 2.30% RSD (Table S3). For mode II nanoprobes, both intra-assay precision and inter-assay accuracy were less than 4%. At last, the stability of this method was verified by detecting S.aureus at 106 cfu/mL in 1, 4, 7, 14, 21 days. According to Fig. S9, the response of this assay toward to S.aureus day to day had little change difference within 21days, and the RSD is 2.5% calculate by the value of I/I
0 .These results indicate that the method has good repeatability, reproducibility and stability.

A comparison of S. aureus detection between this work and other reported methods is displayed in Table S4. As illustrated in Table S4, our proposed sensing strategy may be one of the most sensitive fluorescence assays comparable to previous methods. Besides, the advantages of simple operation and rapid analysis are overwhelming. The entire analysis procedure can be completed within 50 min without any separation and washing steps, including the incubation of nanoprobes with sample (45 min), recording the fluorescence signal by fluorescence spectrometry (5 min).

To investigate the selectivity of our proposed method, five other common bacterial strains including S. typhimurium, L. monocytogenes, E. coli O157:H7, V. parahaemolyticus and S. Bogdii were assessed, and the final concentration of each bacteria sample was adjusted to 105 cfu mL−1. Pure PBS sample was employed as the blank group. As expected, the blank sample and samples contained interfering species exhibit a bright fluorescence intensity due to S. aureus-specific aptamer-triggered disassembly of nanoprobes (Fig. 5 ), and there is no significant difference between blank samples and non-target bacteria (p < 0.05). On the contrary, a remarkable fluorescence decrease in response to the addition of S.aureus was observed, because S.aureus can bind with aptamer and inhibit the disassembly of nanoprobes. Note that mode I strategy had a significantly better performance, because its fluorescence intensity changes between S.aureus-negative and -positive samples is much higher than that of the mode II strategy. In addition, the interference effect of various metal ions, anions and antioxidants on this assay was investigated. 10 mM of Fe2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, CO3 2−, H2PO4 −, CH3COO− (AC−), NO3 −, HCO3-, Na2S2O3, ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH) were added in the reaction system. These above competitive species were dissolved in PBS buffer to a final concentration of 10 mM. Then add these solutions (dissolved in PBS) to the reaction system as an ordinary PBS buffer. S.aureus at 104 cfu·mL−1was used to determine the interference effect. Pure PBS sample was employed as the blank group. According to Fig. S10, all competitive species do not induce obvious I/I
0 changes compared to control under the same conditions (p < 0.05). In general, our proposed assay based on target-inhibited fluorescence signal recovery can achieve selective S.aureus detection without significant interference. These results indicated that the NEST nanoprobe possesses high selectivity for detecting S. aureus.

To further confirm the practical applicability of our proposed method, the milk samples often contaminated by S. aureus were tested. In this study, the samples were collected from a local supermarket, none of S. aureus were detected by the culture method. Then, various concentrations of S. aureus were added to the samples. The calibration curves of milk samples were obtained by spiked with varying concentrations of S. aureus prior to practical analysis (Fig. S11), which eliminated the matrix effect. As summarized in Table 2 , the average recoveries ranged from 81.0 to 102.8% for adopting mode I strategy as well as 88.1–114.5% for adopting mode II strategy, respectively. The relative standard derivations (RSDs) were lower than 10%. These results preliminarily indicate that our methods can be used for accurate quantification of S. aureus in complex food matrices based on their strong anti-interference ability.

In summary, a one-step signal-off fluorometric sensing strategy based on NSET between CDs and AuNPs was developed to detect S. aureus in milk samples. Owing to avoid the use of any kind of enzyme, label, cultivation process, separation and washing steps, or sophisticated equipment, our proposed methods based on two distinct modes of fluorescence quenching approach are simpler and more cost-effective. More importantly, the assay-based fluorescence quenching system with linker DNA has been enhanced detection limit and improved linear range for S. aureus. Therefore, the current study could be implemented as an alternative approach to improve the routine analysis of S. aureus. In this study, we only tested one type of bacteria, but food samples usually contain many kinds of bacteria and other pathogens. So, the detection technology for pathogens should be developed from a single bacterial detection technology to multiple bacterial detection technology. In the future, we will establish an enzyme-free and label-free method in the follow-up work, which will be more rapid and easier to operate for multi-target detection of bacteria, virus and other biomoleculars.

Shuo Yao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Chao Zhao: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Mingyu Shang: Supervision, Software, Validation. Juan Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Juan Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82073603 and 82073557), Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan Item (Grant No. 20200403035SF).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fct.2021.112071.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An immunosensor designed for polybrominated biphenyl detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon dots and gold nanoparticles
